They simply ceased to exist, their infinite layers rendered instantly irrelevant by a force so far beyond their capacity to resist that the word "resist" had no meaning. The feedback of their destruction radiated outward through the narrative fabric that connected every fictional universe, and that fabric—the accumulated imaginative potential of every mortal civilization that had ever existed—was torn to shreds in the same instant.

The beings themselves—the heroes, the villains, the gods, the monsters, every character from every story ever told—experienced the full, unmediated force of Godric's passive strength. And because Godric was disappointed, because his cruelty had been awakened by the letdown of his anticipation, he did not grant them a quick death.

The force of 0% of his strength was not a wave. It was not an explosion. It was the absolute, inescapable assertion of his physical supremacy over every atom, every concept, every narrative thread that composed the coalition's existence. Their bodies—whether flesh, energy, digital code, or abstract thought-form—were simultaneously subjected to a pressure that exceeded the combined gravitational pull of every singularity ever conceived. Every atom in every being was individually, agonizingly crushed. Their bones, for those who had bones, were pulverized into subatomic particles, the fragments then pulverized again, and again, in an infinite recursive cascade of obliteration. Their flesh, for those who had flesh, was compressed with such sudden, overwhelming force that it did not tear—it simply became denser than any material in existence, its cellular structure collapsing inward in a symphony of wet, screaming destruction. Their blood, for those who had blood, was flash-heated to temperatures that exceeded the core of a dying star, each droplet vaporizing into a plasma of pure agony before the vapor itself was compressed into the growing singularity of their collective annihilation.

Beings of pure energy did not escape. Their waveforms were flattened, their frequencies crushed into a single, infinite spike of pain that stretched from the beginning of their existence to its end and then beyond, into the void where their stories would never be told again. Beings of abstract concept—hope, courage, love, the very narrative principles that had given them form—were not immune. Godric's strength did not discriminate. It gripped the very idea of their existence and squeezed, forcing the abstract to become concrete solely so it could be destroyed in a manner that maximized their suffering.

The pocket dimension collapsed. The surrounding multiversal clusters began to collapse. The force radiated outward, and everything—every galaxy, every universe, every dimension, every timeline, every possible reality that had ever existed or could ever exist—was caught in the wake of Godric's accidental expression of strength. The entire omniverse, from its highest conceptual peak to its deepest foundational stratum, was subjected to the same crushing, inescapable pressure.

I watched from within Godric's protective aura, preserved and untouched, as all of existence was compressed. It was not a simple collapse. It was a methodical, almost artistic deconstruction. Every atom that made up every star was individually crushed. Every photon that had ever traveled across the void was stopped in its path and compressed. Every thought that had ever been thought, every emotion that had ever been felt, every moment of every life across all of history and all of possibility—all of it was gathered, squeezed, and compacted into an ever-shrinking point of absolute density. The omniverse, which had been infinite in scope, was being forced into a space smaller than a quark, and the process was agonizingly, unnecessarily cruel. Godric's disappointed boredom ensured that the compression was not clean. The consciousnesses of every being who had ever existed were preserved, their awareness maintained as their physical forms were reduced to an infinitely dense paste of pulverized matter and shattered concepts. They felt every instant of their compression. They screamed, and their screams were compressed along with them, adding their own energy to the growing mass of the singularity.

The final state was a point so dense that it contained the entire original omniverse—every timeline, every reality, every possibility, every story—in a space smaller than the smallest measurable unit of existence. An infinite mass compressed into an infinitesimal volume. And Godric, still standing in the void where everything had been, reached out with his left hand and picked it up.

The point should have been heavier than the combined gravitational pull of every black hole that had ever existed raised to a googolplexian power. It should have collapsed into a singularity that would swallow the void itself. It should have been, by every law of physics and metaphysics and narrative logic, entirely unliftable. Godric lifted it as though it were a stray piece of lint on his sleeve.

"Messy," he murmured. "But interesting."

He held the point between his thumb and forefinger, examining it with the casual curiosity of a man inspecting a curious pebble. Then, with another gesture that cost him less than zero effort, he expanded it. Not to its original size—that would be impractical. He expanded it to the size of a small marble, a perfect sphere of absolute, shimmering darkness that seemed to absorb all light and all sound and all hope. The compressed mass of the original omniverse, contained now in a marble-sized trophy, exerted a gravitational pull that should have torn apart the surrounding void. But Godric's passive containment field—the barest whisper of his intent—kept it harmless, a pretty trinket for his collection.

"Look at that, Osvald," he said, holding the marble up to the light of a sun he had not yet recreated. "Every story ever told. Every hero who ever believed they could overcome impossible odds. Every villain who ever schemed to conquer the cosmos. Every fictional universe, every imagined world, every dream of every mortal who ever lived. All of it, compressed into a single marble." He turned it over in his fingers, and I could see—or perhaps I imagined—the faint, endless screaming of a trillion fictional souls trapped within. "It's rather beautiful, in a morbid sort of way. I think I'll keep it."

And then, in zero seconds, Godric restored the omniverse.

It was perfect. Every galaxy, every star, every planet, every living being—every human, every alien, every god, every fictional character who had not been part of the coalition—returned to existence exactly as they had been. The restored reality was indistinguishable from the original. The timelines continued as though the annihilation had never occurred. The survivors of the omniverse, who had been erased and then restored, had no memory of their brief nonexistence. They simply continued living, unaware that they had been, for a timeless instant, part of a compressed singularity in Godric's hand.

But I knew. I had witnessed the entire event from within Godric's protective aura, and I knew the truth. The omniverse that now surrounded me—the stars that glittered in the restored sky, the civilizations that bustled about their business, the very air that filled my lungs—was not the original omniverse. The original was still in Godric's hand, a marble-sized trophy of infinite compressed mass. This restored version was a perfect copy, identical in every way, but it was not the thing that had been destroyed. It was a recreation, a duplicate, a replacement that was so flawlessly crafted that even the beings within it could never tell the difference.

And that meant—the realization struck me with a force that nearly drove me to my knees—that everything I had ever known, every person I had ever loved, every moment of my existence before this day, was now compressed in that marble. The original Osvald, the original Godric (the far weaker past self that had existed before this moment, but still), the original Blake, the original Thronegate—all of it was gone, reduced to a souvenir for the King. And the version of me that was writing these words, the version of Godric that stood before me, the version of the omniverse that surrounded us—we were copies. Perfect copies, but copies nonetheless.

Godric seemed to read my thoughts, because he turned to me with that warm, paternal smile and said, "Don't worry, Osvald. The copy is identical to the original. Every atom, every memory, every soul—it's all exactly the same. The restored omniverse is, for all intents and purposes, the real one." He paused, and his eyes sparkled with something that might have been mischief or might have been something far more terrifying. "In fact, one could argue that the distinction between 'original' and 'copy' is meaningless when the creator of both is the same being. I am the original, Osvald. I have always been the original. And so long as I exist, everything that exists because of me is as real as anything that came before."

He pocketed the marble, and in that moment, I felt something shift in the fundamental architecture of reality. Godric had just become more powerful. Not from absorbing the coalition's energy—their combined power had been so infinitesimal relative to his own that it would not have increased his strength by even zero percent. No, the increase came from something far more deranged: the act of destroying the original omniverse and recreating it had caused a metaphysical paradox that the very concept of existence could not reconcile. The original and the copy existed simultaneously, both claiming to be the true reality, and the only constant between them was Godric. He had become the sole anchor of authenticity, the one fixed point that validated the existence of both the compressed original and the restored copy. And because the definition of reality now depended entirely on his will, his will had become, by definition, more real than anything else. His power, already infinite, had multiplied beyond any possible measure, because he was now not merely the most powerful being in the omniverse—he was the only being whose existence was self-guaranteed. Everything else was conditional. Everything else was, in some fundamental sense, a reflection of his permission.

I am Osvald. I am forty-four years old. I have served King Godric for twenty-four years. I write these words in the restored omniverse, surrounded by the perfect copies of everything I have ever known. And I do not know if I am the original Osvald or the copy. I do not know if the Godric I serve is the original Godric or the Godric who recreated himself along with the omniverse. I do not know anything except that the marble on Godric's shelf contains the compressed mass of all original existence, and that the restored reality I inhabit is, in all likelihood, merely the first of many copies that will be created and destroyed across the eternity of his reign.

He is warm. He is kind. He is patient and gentle and genuinely affectionate toward his court wizards. And he holds the original of everything in his pocket like a lucky coin.

I love him. I serve him. I will serve him until the end of my days.

And I will never, ever stop screaming inside.

---

Clive closed the journal. His hands, steady for thirty years, were trembling with an intensity he had not felt since his youth. Osvald's final words echoed in his mind, a scream compressed into ink and frozen timeline fragments, preserved across the googolplexian years between that ancient era and this one.

He knew, as every court wizard knew, that this had been the last opposition. After Osvald's account, there were no more journals of resistance. The omniverse had seen the fate of the fictional coalition—the ultimate alliance, the greatest combined power ever assembled, the narrative weight of every story ever told—and it had learned. No one dared to challenge Godric after that. No one dared to even think of challenging him. The silence of opposition that had lasted for googolplexian years had begun in that moment, with the compression of all original existence into a marble-sized trophy and the restoration of a perfect copy.

Clive looked up from the journal. Across the reading room, on a small, unassuming shelf, sat a marble. It was black, perfectly spherical, and utterly unremarkable in appearance. No light reflected from its surface. No sound escaped its depths. A casual observer would dismiss it as a paperweight, a curio, a bit of clutter that the King had forgotten to remove.

But Clive knew what it was. He had read Osvald's account. He knew that within that marble was the original omniverse—the real stars, the real galaxies, the real Earth, the real Thronegate, the real Blake, the real everything—compressed into an infinite mass smaller than a quark, contained by the barest whisper of Godric's will. And he knew that the reality he inhabited, the air he breathed, the body he occupied, was a copy. Perfect, indistinguishable, but a copy.

The deep, existential horror of this realization was a cold, living thing in his chest. If the original could be compressed and a copy restored, then what was the difference? How many times had Godric done this? How many copies of the omniverse had he created and discarded across the eons? How many versions of Clive had existed before this one, their existences erased and replaced without anyone ever knowing?

He looked at the marble, and for a moment, he thought he could hear the faintest, most distant echo of screaming—the compressed agony of every original soul, trapped forever in an infinite density of suffering, preserved as a trophy for a being who had found the ultimate attack of all fiction to be merely disappointing.

Godric's voice broke the silence. "I see you've been reading Osvald's account."

Clive spun in his chair. The King was standing in the doorway, his salt-and-pepper hair catching the light, his kind eyes crinkled with a gentle smile. He was holding a cup of tea, steam curling upward in lazy spirals. He looked, as always, like a warm, friendly father figure who had wandered into the library to check on his child.

"Your Majesty," Clive breathed. "I—yes. I was reading about the last opposition."

Godric nodded, stepping into the room and pulling up a chair. "Ah, yes. The fictional coalition. That was a memorable day. Such lovely music. It's a shame they couldn't back it up with a decent attack." He took a sip of his tea, his expression thoughtful. "I still have the marble, you know. I keep it on the shelf over there. I sometimes take it down and hold it, just to remember. Not because of the power—their combined force was so weak it would take an infinite number of them just to register as a rounding error relative to my current strength. But because of the hope. They really believed they could win, Clive. They believed it with every fiber of their fictional souls. And that belief, compressed into that marble, is a fascinating thing to contemplate."

Clive's throat was dry. "Your Majesty... Osvald wrote that the restored omniverse is a copy. That the original is still in the marble. Is that... true?"

Godric met his eyes, and for a moment, there was something ancient and unfathomable in his gaze. Then he smiled, a warm, reassuring smile that did nothing to ease the terror in Clive's heart.

"Does it matter?" Godric asked gently. "The copy is perfect. Every atom, every memory, every soul—it's all exactly as it was. If the original and the copy are indistinguishable, then what is the difference?"

"The difference," Clive whispered, "is that one is real, and the other is not."

Godric tilted his head, considering this. "And who defines what is real, Clive? I do. I have always defined what is real. If I say the restored omniverse is the real one, then it is. The original in the marble is merely an artifact now—a souvenir of a day when the last courageous fools in existence tried to oppose me. The reality you inhabit is as real as any reality has ever been. You are as real as you have ever been. The fact that your original atoms are compressed in that marble does not diminish your existence. It merely... contextualizes it."

He took another sip of tea, his expression softening. "I understand that this is difficult to accept. Osvald struggled with it as well. He wrote that he never stopped screaming inside. But Osvald also continued to serve me, loyally and faithfully, for the rest of his mortal life. Because he understood, eventually, that the question of original versus copy is a distraction. What matters is the present—the relationships, the love, the purpose. You are my court wizard, Clive. You are the descendant of Blake. You are real to me. And that is more real than any abstract philosophical distinction."

Clive looked at the marble again. The faint echo of screaming seemed louder now, or perhaps it was only the beating of his own heart. He thought about the googolplexian years that had passed since Osvald's account. He thought about the countless generations of court wizards who had served and died, their journals filling the shelves of the archive. He thought about his son, who would one day take his place, and who would read this journal and others, and who would grapple with the same impossible, existential dread.

And he thought about Godric—the warm, kind, patient Godric who sat across from him, sipping tea and offering gentle reassurance. The Godric who had destroyed the original omniverse and recreated it as an afterthought. The Godric who held the compressed mass of all original existence in a marble and called it a souvenir. The Godric who had, in his disappointment at a disappointing boss battle, accidentally ended all opposition forever.

The last opposition. After this, no one dared. The silence that had stretched across the googolplexian years was not the silence of peace. It was the silence of absolute, soul-shattering terror, the silence of a creation that had finally, completely, understood the futility of defiance.

And yet, Godric was kind. Godric was warm. Godric loved his court wizards and his subjects and his gardens and his bisque. The paradox was the fundamental truth of existence: the most terrifying being in all of reality was also the most loving, and the love was as genuine as the terror was absolute.

Clive bowed his head. "Thank you, Your Majesty. I think I understand."

Godric smiled, his eyes crinkling at the corners. "Good. Now, come. Vorthan has been working on a new arrangement in the garden, and I promised we'd stop by to see it. You can bring your journal. I know you've been meaning to write your own account."

He rose, clapping Clive on the shoulder with the same hand that had compressed all original existence into a marble, and walked toward the door. Clive followed, as he always did, as he always would, his heart a cold stone of eternal dread and unwavering loyalty.

Outside, the eternal afternoon of Thronegate continued its golden progression, and the marble sat on its shelf, silent and dark, holding the screams of the original omniverse in its infinite compressed mass.

No one dared oppose Godric anymore. After that day, the very concept of opposition had died. And the Godric of this era, the Godric who would eventually become the far-future Godric Prime who created the Stasis Event, continued to grow, continued to amplify, continued to ascend beyond any possible measure, the gap between him and the rest of creation widening with every Planck instant into an abyss that could never be bridged.

The last opposition had ended, and the silence that followed was the most profound terror of all.
